story:
I always figured if I came back after dying, I’d haunt one of my old haunts—the Court Street Restaurant and Bar, The Madd Hatter, maybe Moran’s, where I nursed Writer’s Tears and chatted with the kind Latina waitress. Surprise: I’m haunting the Hobohemian Public Library across from Church Square Park. Sure, I borrowed books there, but I was an in-and-out guy: hear a book call, check it out, leave. No food, no booze, why linger?

For the record, “to haunt” doesn’t just mean yelling “Boo!” It also means “to frequent a place,” which I did. As for how I died—no drama. I got old, developed COPD, and one day stopped breathing. No angels, no devils, no other ghosts. I didn’t pick this place; like choosing your parents, where you haunt isn’t up to you. Still, as a solitary writer, I don’t mind the quiet. I have endless time and endless shelves.

Ghost mechanics, in case you’re curious: I can’t touch living people, but I can nudge inanimate objects—a book, a chair, the classic bump in the night. I found out something else the hard way. One afternoon I spotted a guy slip a library book under his jacket. I love libraries—free knowledge for everyone—so I rushed him, hoping to knock it loose. I ran right through him. For a heartbeat I felt his thoughts, saw through his eyes—a mess of pain and fear—and he felt me, too. He screamed, dropped the book, and bolted. Ms. Marconi, a staffer, found the book and half-joked, “Guess this place is haunted.”

Nights, I’d read by turning on a single light in a hidden corner. People noticed. The police asked about after-hours lights. A newspaper ran HOBOHEMIAN PUBLIC LIBRARY HAUNTED! Patrons thinned. In came the ghost-hunters with sage and beeping gadgets—mostly clowns and grifters. When they got too noisy, I’d drift through to peek at their thoughts. Then the last one arrived: Gemma. Tall, fiery red hair, calm voice, alone. Real.

“Are you there?” she asked softly. “I don’t want to bother you. I’d just like to know you. One sound for yes, two for no?” I was terrified of scaring her off, but I tipped a chair once. “Thank you,” she whispered. For an hour we traded taps—yes, no, no harm meant—and she pulled a chair close like we were old friends. She wished we could say more.

So I gambled. Instead of rushing through her, I moved slowly, like a hug we both chose. For a breath, we shared thoughts, feelings, memories—hers warm, kind, empathetic. She gasped, tears shining in moonlight. “That felt wonderful. Can we do it again?” We did, carefully, again and again, teaching ourselves a quiet, soul-true way to talk until morning.

When Ms. Marconi arrived, Gemma spoke for me without me ever speaking: “He’s not evil. He’s kind. He’s a local writer who loved this library. Please let him stay.” Marconi sighed. “Alright—if he behaves.” She offered coffee and doughnuts as payment. Gemma only wanted a library card. “So I can come back,” she said. “Sweetheart,” Marconi told her, “it’s a public library. You’re always welcome.”

And me? Bound to stacks and silence, I suddenly wasn’t alone. In this place I used to haunt alive, I’ve found someone who visits by choice. Call me sentimental, but in the hush between page turns and footsteps, I think there’s a ghost of a chance we’re in love.
